sentences.text: Correct.
Well, actually, we were building the outer shell of the factory.
And they had-- what it looked like was a big mountain of gravel.
And then over the gravel was iron rods.
And then they were pouring concrete over them.
And also the foundations-- we were pouring concrete.
The concrete was going day and night on a 24-hour basis without stopping.
And we were put into the-- into the-- into-- to do the work while-- helping the concrete run into the right place.
And we had to stand and stomp it with certain long poles, and carry the iron rods, and connect them-- the rods.
And the work itself was difficult and hard.
However, the Nazis were rushing us constantly.
And they were constantly beating us with electric, electric wires-- electric cables, pieces of electric cable.
They were beating us over the heads in order to expedite and rush us all the time.
They were, like, on a-- on a mission to destroy us and rushing the factory.
And it was mayhem.
We had-- every morning we went to work, we had to carry with us-- at the end of the-- of, of the march-- at the end of the people, there was a wagon-- two wagons, actually, without a horse.
We had to carry-- a few people had to pull the wagons.
And those wagons were so that every single day when we were coming back to camp, people who have died in the interim were put on the wagons and were brought back to the camp.
And that was a daily routine.
They-- food has diminished as they-- time went on, and-- until they were just given a little piece of bread.
They-- the morality of the people was completely shattered to pieces.
I have witnesses-- witnessed father and son actually choking each other because one thought that he got a, a tiny little bit more bread than the other in his ration.
And it was, well, if you want to compare it to animals, I think animals have more instinct than, than, than the way we were meant-- made to feel.
It was-- all reality has disappeared.
You live for that moment and for that little piece of bread that you can get.
As a matter of fact, sometimes I wonder-- I ask myself the question, how the heck did I survive all that?
And what happened to us-- my father and myself, we were still together at that time.
And when we went out to work for the small company, many times we had to work even at night.
There were night shift and day shifts.

And, and the night shift-- when we worked the night shift, we-- the Germans-- not the soldiers, but there were other supervisors and other people-- engineers and other people, civilians-- used to come and supervise the job.
And, and they had their rucksack or the backpack with their lunches and their food.
And they were hanging on a chair because these factories were being built inside the woods.
And I was so starved.
I was so hungry that there were times where I saw that, where he hung up his backpack, and I knew there was food in there.

So I used to crawl on all-- on my belly and get through the line where the German watchmen, the, the police or the, the soldiers were watching, and reach that spot where that was and still, so-- very quietly, open it up and remove a piece of bread or a lump of sugar or, or, or a piece of cheese, and put it back together, so it wouldn't be recognized somehow.
And I did that quite a-- quite a number of times.
And there were others that wanted to do that.
They were caught.
And they were-- they were actually brought to camp and given 25 lashes in front of everybody.
But as a youngster, I don't know, the rationale did not exist.
I knew that it was risky in my mind.
But it didn't enter my mind the, the enormous chances that I took, that all I wanted to do was get hold of that little piece of bread.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that went on for, for about five months like that.

And after-- here, I must also mention that when we used to come back from work in the evening, the German who was in charge over there, he stood there, singing marches, so that everybody marched to his tune.
He enjoyed the fact that he was able to stand there and with his-- both arms, singing a march, some kind of a German march.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they sent us from there-- the-- at one point, after five months in this Camp Number I, they had a roll call.
And they brought in some high-echelon Nazis-- Gestapo, doctors-- that were there.
And they had a selection.
Everybody had to pass by this committee and you were put to the right or to the left.
And the-- those that were already too skinny, too thin-- they called them Muselmann-- and were not fit for this harsh labor, they sent to Camp Number V, Kaufering.
It was in the town, town-- in a village-- small town called Kaufering.
Lager I was in Landsberg.
Lager IV was in Kaufering.
Lager V-- I'm sorry.
I meant Lager V.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that camp, nobody went to work outside.
There was a whole section that was quarantined off.
And I developed-- I was there for a while, actually.
And just being in, in the camp-- they did some work in the camp, some wood chopping, carrying things for the Germans.
But there was no outside work.
And my father was with me.
He got a little job for the kitchen.
He had to go and transport some of the food, the potatoes or something, push the wagon towards the kitchen.
Number 5, yes.
Actually, I believe that I was selected number V.
And, and my father sort of was able to join me somehow undetected.
And there was a section there that was quarantined.
And at one point, I developed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typhoid fever.
And that section was full of people with this type of fever.
And I was laid up in there.
And, for the most part, there were, like, 50 people on this side, 50 people on that side.
And every morning, they removed dozens of people who have died-- every single morning.
My father used to come in the evening, was able to steal his way in, and stood there facing me, with tears running down his face.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He saw me.
He saw that he's losing me.
What he was able to do for me was bring little, like, a-- one potato or something.
And he was able to cut little slices.
And there was a, a, a stove, a metal stove there-- and hold it up against the side of it to cook it and feed me a-- little pieces of potato.
[SIGHS] This is-- people next to me on the left side died one day.
And the people on the right side died another day.
New people were brought in.
I was there approximately a week to 10 days.
I have reached a climax where I was delirious.
And I think I was out of it.
When I woke up, my father was standing there, breathing.
And, and, in a-- sort of a roundabout way, he was sort of, like, talking to himself.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I have passed the crisis.
The fever has subsided.
I've come out of it.
And most people died right there.
Somehow, I survived it.

And when I survived it, I was-- they were able to take me out of there and put me back with my father in the-- in the-- where I came from from that barrack, from that particular building that I was in.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a few months over there, I have-- suddenly, I looked up one day, up in the sky.
And I saw tiny, little specks of planes flying over, which I haven't seen before.
And I, I said to my father, I says, what, what, what is this-- what it is?
He says, I don't know.
Let's see.
And so every single day, there were planes coming, coming over.
And then we heard the explosions, big explosions.
We were not too far from Munich, maybe 20 miles or something.
But we were able to hear faint explosions from far away.
And we have surmised-- because we had-- we had no way of finding what was going on, what the war is like.
So we have surmised that this must be either a Russian or American or British planes bombing Germany.
And that gave us a little bit more of a hope.
Somehow we have renewed hope that something is happening, that perhaps if we can hold on with our last bit of strength, maybe, maybe we can still make it.
There were days where I was outside there.
I was so hungry.
I was so starved that I actually pulled the grass from the foot-- from the ground and chewed on grass.
And I looked up, and I counted the planes.
There were times that I counted up to 400 planes passing by and 500 planes another day.
And the bombings and all that-- I was sure that something is happening-- and just hold on.
And that gave me the idea, oh, God, if I can only survive this thing.
If I can only find myself on the other side a free man and be able to sit at a table, look at a whole loaf of bread, be able to cut off a piece to eat, I'll never ask for more.
I'll, I'll be very satisfied and happy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] That was 1944, and I was 15 years old.
And I had gone through such hell, seen so much.
But now, I was invigorated by what I saw up there in the sky.
And I said, I must hold on, even if it's to be a witness to tell the story, the tragedy of our people.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] That lasted till somewheres in April of 1945.
And at one point, towards the end of April 1945, they told everybody from the camp to get out on a roll call.
And they marched us to the train.
And they put us on a train.
We found out later on that they were trying to get us either to the Alps and kill us because Auschwitz and all the other camps were already bombed out and taken over by the Russian Armies over there.
And a lot of other people, they just marched to the Alps.
The marches where-- most-- a lot of them died right just marching through the-- through the cold nights and days.
They put us on the train.
And on the train-- train took off-- now, the train was boxcars, but there were no roofs on it.
So all the people were put in like sardines.
It was very, very dense, with these striped uniforms that, that we were wearing.
And one day in the morning, we take a look.
We wound up right next to a German train, with-- where every third car had an anti-aircraft cannon, cannon on it.
And sure enough, in a-- in a short while ensued a, a-- we were right in the midst of the war.
In other words, the Germans were trying to camouflage themselves with showing that there were prisoners in the next car.
That's why there were cars without the roofs.
But the United States airplanes-- the fighter bombers came running and making sorties right on the entire length of the train.
And they bombed out the locomotives.
That's the first thing that they bombed out.
And the Germans were using their anti-aircraft guns.
And we were right in the middle.
Oh, I would estimate maybe 30, maybe 25 or 30, something like that.
And the German train was-- it was a big one.
We found out later it was loaded with provisions-- food and clothing.
And they were trying to deliver that to the front line.
But they got caught.
They were bombed out.
And in the midst of everything else there, as the planes were coming down, scooping down an entire length of the train.
A lot of people got killed from the-- prisoners of, of war-- us, the Jews.
And I knew that there was-- we were so dense that there was a whole slaughter going on.
No, no, the Germans said that we can't get out.
Once you get out, you get shot by the planes.
The, the, the, the shells landed on, on some of the-- of our cars as well.
When I saw what was happening-- and there's a lot riding all over the place, all around me-- I jumped over the top.
I lowered myself in.
And I ran around the entire length of the train.
And I opened all the hooks of the doors.
And the people ran out.
And the Germans saw what was happening.
So they encircled a big place.
And they said, OK, you can run into the woods and stay right in that area.
But-- so we ran into that area.
They allowed us to hide from the planes.
The planes kept coming and, and, and, and, and bombarding and shooting.
And there was a lot of blood running right from the boards from the-- from the trains on the bottom.
A lot of people got killed.
And after the planes went away, the Germans forced us back.
And they picked me and some other people to go from car to car and pick out this-- those that were-- people that were killed-- take them out of the train.
There were-- I had to carry fingers, hands, legs that were actually cut right through-- take it off the trains.
They thought-- they were still hoping that they can get us back on the train and take us somewhere.
And then the planes came again and again and again, on one sortie after the other.
We finally-- we were going back and forth to the woods, and back to the cars-- back and forth.
Finally, they didn't-- towards the evening, they didn't let up.
And we were staying in the-- in the woods surrounded by the Germans.
And my father said, you know what?
I think the time has come that maybe we should run away from here.
And we found a moment, and we-- and a place where we ran out, that perimeter that they were watching there.
And we run away into the woods, deeper into the woods.
We're running and running and running.
We were extremely exhausted.
We didn't have the strength.
We didn't have the food.
And we finally reached a spot where we said that we can't go no further.
There were other people that also escaped that moment.
And some of them-- another fellow joined us.
And we reached a moment where we collapsed in the woods.
And we all fell asleep.
We fell asleep.
And the next morning, the rain was tremendous.
And we were awakened by three German soldiers with their rifles at our heads.
And they said that we should not stay here.
We should get out-- get out of here.
We were sure that we were going to get shot.
As it turned out, it was that these German soldiers were running away from the front line themselves.
And so they told us to go in this direction.
Over there, there's a highway.
And there are Americans over there.
But we didn't believe them.
We thought that they were just telling us a story, and they're going to shoot us anyway.
So we ran.
And they did not shoot.
They took off.
But we knew.
We knew that it's happening-- just, just a little longer to hold on.
[SIGHS] And so at that time, it all took a couple of days.
The next night, we-- my father and I, we were wandering.
And night came.
We looked for a place where we can stay overnight.
We found a little cabin from a truck that was stationed-- placed over there.
And we opened that door.
And we got inside.
And we're there through the night.
And through the night, there were artillery cannons.
Artillery was, was going constantly right over our heads.
And as we woke up in the morning, we-- it was silent.
It was total silent-- total quiet.
And so we started walking.
We're just walking.
We didn't know where-- what to, to walk.
We wound up in a little village over there.
And out of the village came out a, a German in a-- he was in a uniform, and-- one tall German.
And he came to us.
And he asked us what we're doing here.
We told him that we had lost our way, and all that.
And he says, you can't come in here.
You can't go here.
He says, you go there-- go away from here and make sure you don't come back again.
And we did.
We walked away.
But as we were walking, somehow, without any direction, without knowing how, we came all around.
And we came back to the same spot.
And he caught us a second time.
He caught us a second time.
And he started shouting and yelling at us.
He took out his pistol.
And he put his pistol right at my temple, over here.
[POINTS] And my father-- my father dropped to the floor, pleading and begging him, with tears in his eyes, please don't shoot him.
He is my son.
Please, we only-- didn't find our way.
And somehow, he said, all right, this is your last chance.
He says, you better go and disappear out of here.
I don't want to see you again.
And so we thanked him.
And I got up.
And we ran with all our last might.
We ran back.
And we thought that we were going away from this place.
But believe it or not, it happened a third time-- where we came around, we lost our way, and he caught us for a third time.
We were sure that we were-- he was going to kill us right then and there.
My father pleaded and begged.
He says, please, if you can only tell us where to go, the direction to go, so we don't have to wind up in here again.
He again took out his pistol and pistol-whipped my father.
He pistol-whipped me.
And he said-- he said to me, this is the third time.
The next time, you will be shot dead.
And he says, you go there.
[POINTING] And he pointed to a certain road over there.
And he said, make sure you go straight with the road.
Don't get off that road.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we did.
We went down the road.
There was no sign of life on the road, just him and me and all our bedclothes there-- the, the dirty clothes-- the filthy clothes that we had.
Somehow or other, I wound up back where the train was bombed out-- my father and I.
It was quiet, silent.
There was nobody around.
And the German train was all bombed out.
The-- some of the, the doors were open.
As we got to the train, we saw that there were provision.
There was food in there, and clothing, and all of that.
And I, I was soaking wet, dirty.
So I decided to go in-- into the car and change my clothes, put on some clean clothes.
I put on something-- underwear.
And there was nothing but the German uniforms there, with, with boots.
So I got in in one of the uniforms and the boots.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father changed some underwear only.
And we find cheese, found the other things, and we took it with us.
And we walked out.
And we continued to-- walking.
And we walked into this village.
And for the first time, we saw the first American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] truck pass us by-- and a few jeeps pass us by.
And one jeep stopped.
And he started talking to me.
And I didn't know what he was saying.
And he pointed that I should go in there.
There was a, a-- like a barn.
And I, I went in there.
And I noticed that it was filled with German soldiers that they collected over there, probably taking them as prisoners of war.
And--
No, my father was left standing outside.
But I was-- but the, the Americans didn't speak any German.
They didn't speak any other language.
So I ran out of there.
And I ran over to the man.
And I started talking to him to in Yiddish and Polish, you know.
I-- no, he says, you go back there.
Finally, he must have sensed something.
So there was another jeep came by.
And there was a captain sitting in front.
He was being driven by his chauffeur on a jeep.
And he calls me-- the other guy-- the other American talks to him.
And he calls me over.
And he starts speaking to me in a broken German.
But I recognized that he also speaks Yiddish.
And I explained myself what happened to me-- I said to him.
Oh my god, he says, after all that, you have to be exposed to this?
So he took me immediately to the next closest house.
He kicked the door open and-- with his rifle.
But there was a woman came out.
And she said-- he said-- told her to open the closet.
He asked if she has a, a husband here, anyone?
She said, no, her husband was killed on the Russian front line.
So he opened-- she says-- he says, open the closet.
Let the man-- give him all the clothes that he wants.
And I got into the civilian clothes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father joined me.
And they put us-- they carried us, actually, until the ambulances came.
They put my father and me and other stragglers that they found over there into the ambulances.
And they took us to a place called Sankt Ottilien.
Sankt Ottilien is a church-- a former church, which was converted into a hospital during the war for the German soldiers.
Sankt Ottilien?
Well, 'Sankt' is S-T period. 'Ottilien' is O-T-T-I-L-A-N, I believe, Sankt Ottilien.
